title: Contracts & Sales
subject: Contracts
summary: Contracts and Sales covers formation, offer and acceptance, consideration, defenses, Statute of Frauds, parol evidence, performance, breach, remedies, and UCC sales rules.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Contracts is about promises that the law will enforce.
The main MBE questions are usually:
What damages or remedy is available?
Is this common law or UCC?
Common law = services and land. UCC = sale of goods.

Common law = services and land. UCC = sale of goods.
- UCC Article 2 governs sales of movable goods.
- Common law governs services, land, employment, insurance, and intangible rights.

Rule: A contract generally requires offer, acceptance, consideration, and no valid defense.
Consideration: Bargained-for legal benefit or detriment.
- Past consideration is generally not consideration.
- A preexisting legal duty is generally not new consideration.
Exam tip: Separate contract formation from promissory-estoppel enforcement.
Rule: An offer requires objective present intent to contract, reasonably definite terms, and communication to the offeree.
- Advertisements are usually invitations to negotiate.
- An advertisement may be an offer when definite, specific, and leaving nothing open for negotiation.
Exam tip: Ask whether a reasonable recipient would believe acceptance alone forms a contract.
Common law: Essential terms generally include parties, subject matter, quantity, price, and time.
UCC: Quantity is ordinarily essential; other terms may be supplied by gap fillers.
Requirements and output contracts: Valid when quantities are determined in good faith and are not unreasonably disproportionate to estimates or prior dealings.
Exam tip: The UCC tolerates open terms more readily than common law.
- Revocation is generally effective upon receipt.
- Indirect revocation occurs when the offeree learns from a reliable source that the offeror took definite inconsistent action.

- Beginning performance of a unilateral contract.
3. Assurance the offer will remain open.
4. Without consideration, maximum irrevocability is three months.
Exam tip: An option and a firm offer are different doctrines.
Rule: Acceptance must be made by the proper offeree in a manner invited or reasonably permitted by the offer.
Mailbox rule: Acceptance is generally effective upon dispatch.
- Option-contract acceptance is effective upon receipt.
- Rejection sent first and relied upon before acceptance arrives.
Silence: Generally not acceptance unless prior dealings, agreed circumstances, or exercise of dominion makes it reasonable.
Exam tip: Identify whether the contract is bilateral or unilateral.
Common law: Acceptance must mirror the offer; a material change is a counteroffer.
UCC: A definite expression of acceptance may create a contract despite additional or different terms unless acceptance is expressly conditioned on assent to the new terms.
Between merchants, additional terms generally become part of the contract unless:
1. Offer limits acceptance to its terms.
2. New term materially alters the contract.
Conduct: If writings do not form a contract but parties perform, agreed terms plus UCC gap fillers govern.
Exam tip: Do not apply the mirror-image rule to a sale of goods.
Common law: Generally requires new consideration.
UCC: No new consideration, but modification must be made in good faith.
Statute of Frauds: Modification must be written if the contract as modified falls within the statute.
Exam tip: Determine governing law before asking whether consideration is needed.
Writing: Must generally identify the transaction’s essential terms and be signed by the party to be charged.
- Goods: specially manufactured goods, judicial admission, accepted payment or goods, and merchant confirmation.
Exam tip: The one-year rule applies only when performance is impossible within one year from formation.
Rule: Put the injured party in the position the party would have occupied if the contract had been performed.
Formula: Loss in value plus incidental and consequential losses minus costs and losses avoided.
Key distinction: Punitive damages are generally unavailable for ordinary breach.
Exam tip: Separate expectation, reliance, restitution, incidental, and consequential damages.
Rule: A final written agreement may bar evidence of earlier or contemporaneous agreements.
Completely integrated writing: Bars contradictory and consistent additional terms.
Partially integrated writing: Bars contradictory terms but may allow consistent additional terms.
Exam tip: The rule does not exclude evidence of conduct or agreements occurring after execution.

sections.title: 1. Predominant Purpose Test
body: If a contract involves both goods and services, use the predominant-purpose test.
Is the main purpose goods or services?
UCC applies to the whole contract.
Common law applies, but UCC rules may apply to goods-related parts.
Maya hires a company to install custom kitchen cabinets.
If the main point is buying cabinets, UCC may apply.
If the main point is skilled installation and design, common law may apply.
Mixed contract = ask what the deal is mainly about.

sections.title: 3. Objective Theory of Contracts
body: What would a reasonable person think the words or conduct meant?
The court usually does not care about secret thoughts.
“I will sell you my laptop for $600. Say yes by Friday.”
Maya reasonably believes Leo is serious.
If Maya knew or should have known Leo was joking, then no contract.
Contract intent is judged by outward words and conduct, not secret thoughts.

sections.title: 4. Offer
body: An offer is a statement or act showing willingness to enter a deal that gives the other person power to accept.
Could the other person reasonably think, “If I say yes, we have a deal”?
“I will sell you my desk for $300.”
Offer = yes would close the deal.

sections.title: 6. Missing Terms Under the UCC
body: The UCC may fill missing terms if the parties intended to contract and there is a basis for a remedy.
Maya agrees to sell Leo 50 lamps but they do not mention price.
If they intended to contract, the court can supply a reasonable price.
UCC gap fillers save goods contracts.

body: Missing terms can sometimes be filled.
“I’ll sell you some nice furniture for a fair amount.”
The court may not know what the parties agreed to.
Missing terms can be filled. Vague terms can kill the deal.

sections.title: 8. Advertisements
body: Advertisements are usually invitations to make offers, not offers.
Usually, the customer makes the offer by trying to buy.
An ad can be an offer if it is clear, definite, and limited.
“One signed guitar, $500, first person to arrive at 9 a.m. gets it.”
Ads usually invite offers unless they are very specific and limited.

sections.title: 9. Invitation to Deal
body: A statement that merely starts negotiations is not an offer.
That is probably negotiation, not a firm offer.
Negotiation language is not an offer.

sections.title: 10. Termination of Offers
body: Offer must still be alive to accept.

blocks.text: Offer must still be alive to accept.

sections.title: 11. Lapse of Time
body: If the offer states a deadline, that deadline controls.
If no deadline is stated, the offer ends after a reasonable time.

If parties are talking in person or on the phone, the offer usually ends when the conversation ends unless stated otherwise.
No deadline = reasonable time. Conversation offer usually ends with conversation.

sections.title: 12. Death or Incapacity
body: An offer usually terminates if the offeror or offeree dies or becomes mentally incapacitated before acceptance.
An option contract does not terminate because of death or incapacity.
Maya offers to sell Leo a painting.
Death kills ordinary offers, not options.

sections.title: 14. Constructive Revocation
body: An offer is revoked if the offeree learns reliable information that the offeror took definite action inconsistent with the offer.
Maya offers to sell Leo her piano.
Before Leo accepts, Leo learns from a reliable source that Maya already sold the piano to Nora.
Reliable news that the offeror sold it can revoke the offer.

body: Some offers cannot be revoked for a period of time.

sections.title: 16. Option Contract
body: An option is a promise to keep an offer open.
Common law usually requires consideration for the option.
Leo pays Maya $50 to keep her offer to sell a guitar open for 10 days.
Maya cannot revoke during those 10 days.
Option = paid-for promise to keep offer open.

sections.title: 17. UCC Firm Offer
body: A merchant’s signed written promise to keep an offer open is irrevocable without consideration.
If no time is stated, reasonable time.
But without consideration, irrevocability cannot exceed 3 months.
A furniture merchant signs a letter saying:
“This offer to sell 200 chairs will remain open for 60 days.”
The merchant cannot revoke for 60 days.
UCC firm offer = merchant + signed writing + keep-open promise.

sections.title: 18. Promissory Estoppel Making Offer Irrevocable
body: If the offeree reasonably and foreseeably relies on the offer before acceptance, the offer may become irrevocable to avoid injustice.
A subcontractor gives a contractor a bid.
The contractor relies on that bid to submit a larger project bid.
The subcontractor may not be able to revoke after reliance.
Reliance can lock an offer open.

sections.title: 19. Unilateral Contract and Partial Performance
body: For a unilateral contract, once the offeree starts performance, the offer becomes temporarily irrevocable.
The offeree gets a reasonable time to finish.
But the contract is not accepted until performance is complete.
“I will pay $500 to whoever paints my fence.”
Maya cannot revoke once Leo starts.
But Leo does not accept until he completes the painting.
Unilateral contract = start locks offer, finish accepts.

sections.title: 20. Rejection
body: An offeree’s rejection ends the offer.
Maya offers to sell Leo a desk.
Rejection kills the offer when received.

blocks.text: An offeree’s rejection ends the offer.

sections.title: 21. Counteroffer
body: A rejection of the original offer, and
Maya offers to sell a desk for $300.
Leo rejected the $300 offer and made a new $250 offer.

sections.title: 22. Mere Inquiry
body: A question is usually not a counteroffer.
Maya offers to sell a desk for $300.
That may be a mere inquiry.
The original offer may still be open.

blocks.text: A question is usually not a counteroffer.

sections.title: 23. Acceptance
body: Acceptance is an objective manifestation of assent to the offer.
Only the person invited to accept can accept.
The offeree must know about the offer.
Maya offers Leo $100 to find her missing watch.
Leo finds it without knowing about the reward.
Leo cannot accept the offer because he did not know about it.
You cannot accept an offer you do not know about.

sections.title: 24. Bilateral Contract
body: A bilateral contract is promise for promise.
“I promise to pay you $500 if you promise to clean my garage.”

sections.title: 25. Unilateral Contract
body: A unilateral contract is promise for performance.
“I will pay $500 if you clean my garage.”
Leo accepts only by actually cleaning the garage.

sections.title: 26. Silence as Acceptance
body: Offeree intended silence to accept and had reason to know silence could accept; or
Prior dealings make it reasonable to require the offeree to speak up.
Maya regularly orders coffee beans from Leo every Monday.
Leo sends the usual shipment and invoice.
Maya keeps the shipment silently as usual.
Silence may operate as acceptance because of prior dealings.
Silence usually means nothing unless conduct or history makes it acceptance.

Shipping wrong goods = acceptance plus breach, unless clearly an accommodation.

sections.title: 28. Mailbox Rule
body: An acceptance sent by proper mail is effective when sent, not when received.
Maya offers Leo a contract by mail.
The contract forms Monday, even if Maya receives it Wednesday.
Mailbox rule usually applies only to acceptance.

sections.title: 29. Mailbox Rule Exceptions
body: Mailbox Rule Does Not Apply To
Offers requiring receipt by a deadline
Leo has an option to buy Maya’s painting by Friday.
Leo mails acceptance Friday, but Maya receives it Monday.
Too late unless the option allowed mailing.

sections.title: 30. Acceptance Sent Before Rejection
body: If acceptance is sent first, then rejection is sent later, acceptance usually controls.
If offeror receives rejection first and reasonably relies on it, offeree may be estopped.

sections.title: 31. Rejection Sent Before Acceptance
body: If rejection is sent first, then acceptance is sent later, whichever arrives first controls.
Leo changes mind and mails acceptance Tuesday.
Rejection first, then acceptance = first received wins.

sections.title: 32. Mirror Image Rule
body: Acceptance must match the offer exactly.
Any different or additional term is a counteroffer.
Maya offers to clean Leo’s office for $500.
“I accept, but only if you also clean the windows.”
Common law acceptance must mirror the offer.

sections.title: 33. UCC Battle of the Forms
body: Under the UCC, an acceptance can still form a contract even if it contains additional or different terms.
If One Party Is Not a Merchant
They become part of the contract only if the offeror separately accepts them.
Additional terms usually become part of the contract unless:
The offer expressly limits acceptance to its terms; or
The offeror objects within a reasonable time.
Between merchants, extra terms usually enter unless surprise, limit, or objection.

sections.title: 34. Material Alteration
body: A term materially alters a contract if it would cause surprise or hardship.

blocks.text: A term materially alters a contract if it would cause surprise or hardship.

sections.title: 35. Different Terms Under UCC
body: Many courts use the knock-out rule.
Different terms knock each other out.
Maya’s form says disputes go to New York courts.
Leo’s form says disputes go to California courts.
Those different terms may knock each other out.
Different terms may knock each other out.

sections.title: 36. Consideration
body: Maya promises to pay Leo $200 if Leo paints her bookshelf.
Consideration = you give something to get something.

blocks.text: Maya promises to pay Leo $200 if Leo paints her bookshelf.

sections.title: 37. Gift Promises
body: A promise to make a gift usually lacks consideration.
“I will give you $1,000 next month because you are my friend.”
Promissory estoppel may apply if there is reasonable reliance.
Gift promise is usually not enforceable without reliance.

blocks.text: A promise to make a gift usually lacks consideration.

sections.title: 38. Adequacy of Consideration
body: Courts usually do not care if the bargain is economically equal.
Maya sells Leo an old comic book for $5.
The comic later turns out to be worth $5,000.
Courts police bargains, not bargain quality.

sections.title: 39. Preexisting Duty Rule
body: Doing what you are already legally obligated to do is not consideration.
“I’ll pay you the $1,000 if you promise to give me your bike.”
Leo gave no new consideration because he already owed the $1,000.
Existing duty is not new consideration.

sections.title: 40. Preexisting Duty Exception: Third Party
body: A promise to perform an existing duty can be consideration if made to a new third party.
Leo already promised Maya he would paint Maya’s office.
Nora promises Leo $300 if Leo completes the painting.
Leo’s performance can be consideration for Nora’s promise.
Existing duty to one person can be new consideration to another.

Existing duty to one person can be new consideration to another.

sections.title: 41. Past Consideration
body: Past acts usually are not consideration.
“Because you helped me, I promise to pay you $500.”
Past help is not bargained-for consideration.

blocks.text: Past acts usually are not consideration.

sections.title: 42. Material Benefit Rule
body: A promise to pay for a material benefit already received may be enforceable to prevent injustice.
Leo finds Maya’s lost laptop in the rain, protects it, and spends money repairing water damage.
Maya later promises to reimburse Leo.
A court may enforce the promise up to the value of the benefit.
No enforcement if the benefit was meant as a gift or the promise is wildly disproportionate.
Past benefit plus later promise may be enforced if needed to avoid injustice.

sections.title: 43. Contract Modification
body: Modification needs good faith, not consideration.
Maya contracts with Leo to paint a house for $3,000.
Leo later demands $4,000 to do the same work.
No new consideration unless something changes.
Maya contracts to sell Leo 500 chairs.
Maya and Leo in good faith agree to a higher price.
Common law modification needs consideration. UCC modification needs good faith.

Maya and Leo in good faith agree to a higher price.
Common law modification needs consideration. UCC modification needs good faith.

sections.title: 44. No Oral Modification Clause
body: A written no-oral-modification clause usually does not prevent later oral modification unless the modified contract falls within the Statute of Frauds.
A no-oral-modification clause is generally enforceable.
UCC respects no-oral-modification clauses more than common law.

sections.title: 45. Accord and Satisfaction
body: Accord and satisfaction means the parties agree to accept different performance to settle an existing duty.
Maya agrees to accept Leo’s bicycle instead.
Delivery of the bicycle is satisfaction.
Maya may sue on the original contract or the accord.
Accord = new deal. Satisfaction = doing the new deal.

blocks.text: Accord and satisfaction means the parties agree to accept different performance to settle an existing duty.

Accord = new deal. Satisfaction = doing the new deal.

sections.title: 46. Payment in Full Check
body: If a claim is disputed and debtor sends a check marked “payment in full,” cashing it may create accord and satisfaction.
Leo honestly disputes and says he owes $700.
Leo sends a $700 check marked “payment in full.”
If Maya cashes it, her claim may be discharged.
Cashing a “payment in full” check can settle a disputed debt.

sections.title: 47. Illusory Promise
body: An illusory promise is not consideration because it does not really promise anything.
“I will buy your desk if I feel like it.”
A promise based on good faith or reasonable satisfaction may be enforceable.
“I will buy the desk if I am honestly satisfied with its condition.”

sections.title: 48. Requirements and Output Contracts
body: Buyer agrees to buy all needed goods from seller.
Seller agrees to sell all produced goods to buyer.
These are valid because good faith limits quantity.
Maya’s bakery agrees to buy all flour it needs from Leo.
Maya cannot suddenly demand 100 times her normal amount in bad faith.
Requirements and output contracts are valid because good faith controls quantity.

sections.title: 49. Settlement of Legal Claim
body: Giving up a legal claim can be consideration if the claim is honestly disputed or doubtful.
The settlement is supported by consideration.
Compromise of a real dispute is consideration.

blocks.text: Giving up a legal claim can be consideration if the claim is honestly disputed or doubtful.

sections.title: 50. Promises Enforceable Without Consideration
body: Promise to pay debt barred by statute of limitations
Some promises are enforced even without consideration.

blocks.text: Promise to pay debt barred by statute of limitations

sections.title: 51. Promissory Estoppel
body: A promise may be enforceable without consideration if:
Reliance is reasonable and foreseeable; and
Injustice can be avoided only by enforcement.
“I will pay your coding bootcamp tuition.”

blocks.text: A promise may be enforceable without consideration if:

sections.title: 52. Charitable Subscriptions
body: Many courts enforce written charitable promises even without proof of reliance.
Maya signs a pledge promising $10,000 to a nonprofit library.
The nonprofit may enforce it even without proving reliance in some jurisdictions.
Charitable pledge may be enforceable without normal reliance proof.

blocks.text: Many courts enforce written charitable promises even without proof of reliance.

sections.title: 53. Void, Voidable, and Unenforceable
body: No legal contract from the start.
Valid unless one party avoids it.
Valid contract exists, but court will not enforce it.
Oral land contract without exception = unenforceable.
Void = nothing. Voidable = can cancel. Unenforceable = valid but cannot sue.

sections.title: 54. Formation Defenses
body: Formation defenses attack whether a real enforceable agreement was fairly made.

blocks.text: Formation defenses attack whether a real enforceable agreement was fairly made.

sections.title: 55. Mutual Mistake
body: A contract may be voidable if:
Mistake materially affected the deal; and
Affected party did not assume the risk.
Maya agrees to sell Leo a painting.
Both believe it is a print.
It is actually an original worth far more.
A court may allow avoidance if the mistake is basic and material.
Mutual mistake = both wrong about a basic fact.

sections.title: 56. Unilateral Mistake
body: If only one party is mistaken, contract usually remains enforceable.
Other party knew, should have known, caused, or failed to disclose the mistake.
Owner sees the bid is obviously too low and tries to accept quickly.
Contractor may be able to avoid.
One-sided mistake usually loses unless the other side knew or enforcement is unfair.

sections.title: 57. Misunderstanding
body: A misunderstanding occurs when both parties attach different meanings to a material term.
Contract uses the innocent party’s meaning.
Maya agrees to sell Leo “the blue car.”
Maya means the dark blue car.
Leo means the light blue car.
If neither has reason to know, no contract.
Same word, different meanings can stop contract formation.

sections.title: 58. Misrepresentation
body: A contract may be voidable if one party justifiably relied on a material or fraudulent misrepresentation.
Maya sells Leo a phone and says:
Maya knows it was repaired twice.
Misrepresentation = false fact that induces agreement.

sections.title: 59. Fraud in the Factum vs. Fraud in the Inducement
body: The person does not know what kind of document they are signing.
The person knows they are contracting but is lied to about facts.
Maya tells Leo he is signing a birthday card, but it is actually a contract.
Maya tells Leo the car has a new engine when it does not.
Factum = tricked about document. Inducement = tricked into deal.

sections.title: 60. Nondisclosure
body: Silence can equal misrepresentation if disclosure is needed to:
Correct a basic mistake where silence is bad faith;
Correct misunderstanding about the writing; or
Maya says the apartment has “no known plumbing problems.”
Before signing, she learns the pipes are failing.
Silence can be fraud when honesty requires speaking.

sections.title: 61. Undue Influence
body: Undue influence is unfair persuasion that overcomes a person’s free judgment.
Maya pressures Leo into selling her his valuable watch for almost nothing.
Undue influence = unfair pressure in a trust/dependence relationship.

sections.title: 62. Duress
body: Duress is an improper threat that leaves no reasonable alternative.
Maya threatens to falsely report Leo to police unless Leo signs a contract.
Duress = improper threat + no meaningful choice.

sections.title: 63. Capacity
body: Some people lack legal capacity to contract.
Capacity asks whether the person had legal ability to be bound.

blocks.text: Some people lack legal capacity to contract.
Capacity asks whether the person had legal ability to be bound.

sections.title: 64. Minors
body: A minor’s contract is voidable by the minor, not by the adult.
Minor may ratify after reaching adulthood.
Minor must pay reasonable value for necessities.
Leo, age 17, buys a luxury speaker.
But if Leo buys necessary food, he must pay reasonable value.
Minor can usually cancel, but must pay reasonable value for necessities.

sections.title: 65. Mental Incapacity
body: If a person has been adjudicated incompetent, contracts are void.
If not adjudicated, contract is voidable if person could not understand the transaction or act reasonably and the other party had reason to know.
Adjudicated incompetent = void. Otherwise maybe voidable.

sections.title: 66. Intoxication
body: A contract is voidable if intoxicated party could not understand the nature and consequences of the transaction and the other party had reason to know.
Leo is visibly intoxicated and does not understand he is selling his car.
Maya knows this and gets him to sign.
Intoxication works only when serious and known.

body: Enforcement defenses say: even if formed, court should not enforce.

blocks.text: Enforcement defenses say: even if formed, court should not enforce.

sections.title: 68. Illegality
body: If the contract requires illegal performance or illegal consideration, it is unenforceable.
Maya pays Leo to hack a competitor’s website.
If contract becomes illegal after formation, duty to perform is discharged.

blocks.text: If the contract requires illegal performance or illegal consideration, it is unenforceable.

sections.title: 69. Licensing Violations
body: If a required license is regulatory, unlicensed person may not enforce the contract.
If license is only revenue-raising, contract may still be enforceable.
Leo performs electrical work without a safety license required to protect the public.
Leo may not be able to enforce the contract.
Regulatory license violation can block recovery.

sections.title: 70. Unconscionability
body: A court may refuse to enforce a contract or term that is extremely unfair when made.
Unconscionability = unfair process or unfair terms.

sections.title: 71. Public Policy
body: A contract may be unenforceable if it violates important public policy.
Public policy can stop enforcement even if contract is not technically illegal.

blocks.text: A contract may be unenforceable if it violates important public policy.

sections.title: 72. Implied-in-Fact Contract
body: A contract can be formed by conduct, not words.
Maya walks into a barbershop, sits in the chair, and asks for a haircut.
She impliedly agrees to pay the usual price.

sections.title: 73. Quasi-Contract
body: Quasi-contract is not a real contract.
It lets plaintiff recover to prevent unjust enrichment.
Defendant knowingly accepted or emergency justified no chance to decline; and
Retention without payment would be unfair.
Leo is unconscious after an accident.
Quasi-contract = pay for benefit when keeping it free would be unfair.

sections.title: 74. Express Warranty
body: An express warranty is created by seller’s promise, fact statement, description, sample, or model that becomes part of the bargain.
That can be an express warranty.
“This is the best jacket in the world.”
That is likely opinion or puffery.
Express warranty = factual promise about goods.

sections.title: 75. Implied Warranty of Merchantability
body: A merchant seller impliedly warrants goods are fit for ordinary use.
Seller must deal in goods of that kind.
A shoe store sells running shoes.
The shoes should be fit for ordinary running use.
Merchantability = ordinary-use promise from merchant seller.

Fitness = special purpose + seller knows + buyer relies.

body: A duty to perform may be discharged if an event or rule excuses performance.
Discharge = duty to perform goes away.

body: Performance may be excused if an unexpected event makes performance extremely difficult or impossible.
Party seeking discharge not at fault
Extreme unexpected event makes performance impracticable
A normal cost increase is usually not enough.
Maya agrees to host an event at a specific hall.
Before the event, the hall burns down without fault.
Impracticability = unexpected event makes performance extremely difficult or impossible.

The city cancels the fireworks for safety reasons.
Frustration = performance possible, but point of deal is gone.

sections.title: 83. Release
body: A release is a writing showing intent to discharge another party’s duty.
A written signed waiver or renunciation can discharge without consideration.
Release = written letting-go of a duty.

sections.title: 84. Destruction of Identified Goods
body: If identified goods are destroyed without fault before risk of loss passes, contract is avoided.
Buyer may accept at reduced price or avoid.
Maya contracts to sell Leo a specific antique chair.
Before risk passes, the chair is destroyed in a fire without fault.
Specific goods destroyed before risk passes = contract avoided.

sections.title: 85. Third-Party Beneficiaries
body: A third-party beneficiary contract exists when contracting parties intend to benefit someone who is not a party to the contract.
Intended beneficiary can sue. Incidental beneficiary cannot.

blocks.text: A third-party beneficiary contract exists when contracting parties intend to benefit someone who is not a party to the contract.

sections.title: 86. Intended Beneficiary
body: A third party is intended if the contract shows intent to give that person the benefit of performance.
Maya pays Leo to deliver a birthday cake to Nora.
Intended beneficiary = contract was meant to benefit them.

blocks.text: A third party is intended if the contract shows intent to give that person the benefit of performance.

sections.title: 87. Creditor Beneficiary
body: A creditor beneficiary benefits because performance satisfies a debt owed by the promisee.
Maya pays Leo to give Nora $500.
Creditor beneficiary = third party gets paid because promisee owes them.

blocks.text: A creditor beneficiary benefits because performance satisfies a debt owed by the promisee.

sections.title: 88. Donee Beneficiary
body: A donee beneficiary benefits because promisee wants to make a gift.
Maya pays Leo to give Nora a new laptop as a gift.

blocks.text: A donee beneficiary benefits because promisee wants to make a gift.

sections.title: 89. Incidental Beneficiary
body: An incidental beneficiary benefits from the contract but was not intended to receive legal rights.
Maya hires Leo to renovate her storefront.
Nearby shops get more foot traffic because the area looks nicer.
Those shops are incidental beneficiaries and cannot sue.

blocks.text: An incidental beneficiary benefits from the contract but was not intended to receive legal rights.

sections.title: 90. Vesting of Beneficiary Rights
body: Once rights vest, original parties cannot modify or rescind without beneficiary’s consent.
Assents at a party’s request; or
Nora is intended beneficiary of a tuition payment.
Nora enrolls in school relying on the promise.
Once beneficiary relies, assents, or sues, rights vest.

sections.title: 91. Promisor Defenses Against Beneficiary
body: Promisor can raise defenses against beneficiary that promisor had against promisee.
Maya promises Leo she will pay Nora if Leo paints Maya’s office.
Maya can raise Leo’s breach as a defense.
Beneficiary stands in promisee’s shoes against promisor defenses.

sections.title: 92. Assignment
body: Most contract rights can be assigned.
Maya has a right to receive $1,000 from Leo.
Maya assigns that right to Nora.

sections.title: 93. Limits on Assignment
body: Assignment Is Not Allowed If It:
Materially increases obligor’s duty or risk;
Materially reduces chance of performance; or
Leo contracts to paint Maya’s portrait.
Maya cannot assign her right to a portrait to Nora if Leo’s performance depends on Maya’s appearance.
Assignment cannot unfairly change the obligor’s burden.

body: Assignment may still work, but assignor breaches.

Paid assignment sticks. Gift assignment may be revocable.

Assignee also takes subject to defenses against assignor.

If Maya had breached the contract, Leo may raise that defense against Nora.
Assignee gets the right, but also takes the baggage.

Special person, special skill, or contract ban can block delegation.

body: A novation substitutes a new party and releases the old party.

Novation = new obligor, old obligor released.

body: Certain contracts must be evidenced by a signed writing to be enforceable.

blocks.text: Certain contracts must be evidenced by a signed writing to be enforceable.

sections.title: 102. Statute of Frauds Writing
body: Be signed by party to be charged;
Show a contract was made; and

Maya’s main purpose is her own business benefit.

Payment + possession + improvements can rescue oral land contract.

sections.title: 113. Promissory Estoppel and Statute of Frauds
body: A promise may be enforced despite Statute of Frauds if reliance makes nonenforcement unjust.

Leo spends major money preparing the space because of the promise.
Reliance can sometimes beat Statute of Frauds.

body: Parol evidence rule limits evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements that contradict a final written contract.
Parol evidence rule protects final writings from earlier contradictory terms.

sections.title: 115. Integration
body: An integrated writing is the final expression of the parties’ agreement.
No prior or contemporaneous terms may be added or contradicted.
Final as to some terms, not all.
Consistent additional terms may be admitted.
Total integration blocks more evidence than partial integration.

body: A merger clause says the writing is final and complete.
“This agreement is the complete and final agreement between the parties.”

sections.title: 120. Conditions
body: A condition is a future event that must happen before a duty arises or is discharged.
Failure of condition means duty may not arise.
Condition controls duty. Promise creates duty.

sections.title: 124. Condition Subsequent
body: An event that cuts off an existing duty.

Losing the license ends Maya’s duty to employ Leo.
Condition subsequent = duty existed, then event ends it.

body: Most contracts use reasonable person standard.

Taste = honest subjective satisfaction. Utility = reasonable person satisfaction.

Substantial performance = close enough to trigger payment, minus damages.

sections.title: 128. Material Breach
body: A material breach means the nonbreaching party did not receive the substantial benefit of the bargain.
Nonbreaching party may withhold performance and sue.
Material breach = big enough breach to excuse the other side.

body: Intentional major deviation is less likely to be substantial performance.

Leo intentionally installs cheaper laminate to save money.
Willful wrong performance is less likely substantial performance.

UCC perfect tender = goods must match exactly.

body: Seller can cure defective tender if:
Time for performance has not expired; or
Seller reasonably believed buyer would accept the nonconforming goods.
Seller must give notice and provide conforming goods.
Seller ships wrong size shirts before deadline.
Seller still has time to ship correct size.
If time remains, seller usually can fix.

body: Perfect tender does not apply to installment contracts.
Buyer can reject an installment only if nonconformity substantially impairs that installment and cannot be cured.
Buyer can cancel whole contract only if nonconformity substantially impairs the entire contract.

One shipment is slightly late but still usable.
Maya likely cannot cancel the whole contract.

Buyer usually gets to inspect before accepting.

Rejection needs timely notice before acceptance.

Defect was hard to discover or seller gave assurances.
Must revoke within reasonable time and before substantial change not caused by defect.
It has a hidden defect that causes failure after two weeks.
Leo may revoke acceptance if value is substantially impaired.
Revocation = undo acceptance for serious hidden or uncured defect.

body: Every contract includes duty of good faith and fair dealing.
Parties must act honestly and fairly in performance and enforcement.

She cannot reject it dishonestly just to avoid paying.

body: A party can waive a condition by words or conduct.
A waived condition may sometimes be reinstated if the other party has not relied.

Maya may be estopped from insisting on original date.
Waived condition may be gone if the other side relies.

sections.title: 140. Prevention Doctrine
body: If a party wrongfully prevents a condition from occurring, the condition is excused.
Maya promises to pay Leo if Maya’s inspector approves Leo’s work.
Maya blocks the inspector from entering.
Maya cannot use lack of approval as a defense.
You cannot block the condition and then complain it did not happen.

body: Once a duty to perform exists, failure to perform is breach unless duty is discharged.

Minor breach gives damages but other party must still perform.
Seller usually must perfectly tender conforming goods.
Common law asks material breach. UCC asks perfect tender.

body: Anticipatory repudiation occurs when a party clearly and unequivocally says or shows they will not perform before performance is due.

Treat as breach and sue now;
Anticipatory repudiation = clear early refusal to perform.

Repudiation can be taken back until the other side treats it as final.

Maya requests written assurance Leo can still deliver.

Expectation = what performance would have been worth.

Maya breaches and Leo loses conference profits.
Those lost profits may be recoverable if foreseeable and proven.
Consequential damages require known special circumstances.

Amount was reasonable when contract was made.

It does not create a separate lawsuit by breaching party.

Leo must make reasonable efforts to find similar work.
Mitigation = do not let damages pile up unreasonably.

Dealer still lost one sale because it could have sold to both buyers.
